### [Privacy Policy](https://searchwp.com/privacy-policy/)

**Published:** September 10, 2014
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

Your privacy is very important to us. At SearchWP we have a few fundamental principles that we follow:

- We donât ask you for personal information unless we truly need it. (We canât stand services that ask you for things like your gender or income level for no apparent reason.)
- We donât share your personal information with anyone except to comply with the law, develop our products, or protect our rights.
- We donât store personal information on our servers unless required for the on-going operation of our site.

SearchWP LLC (âSearchWPâ) operates several websites including SearchWP.com. It is SearchWPâs policy to respect your privacy regarding any information we may collect while operating our websites.

**Website Visitors**

Like most website operators, SearchWP collects non-personally-identifying information of the sort that web browsers and servers typically make available, such as the browser type, language preference, referring site, and the date and time of each visitor request. SearchWPâs purpose in collecting non-personally identifying information is to better understand how SearchWPâs visitors use its website. From time to time, SearchWP may release non-personally-identifying information in the aggregate, e.g., by publishing a report on trends in the usage of its website.

SearchWP also collects potentially personally-identifying information like Internet Protocol (IP) addresses for logged in users and for users leaving comments on our blogs. SearchWP only discloses logged in user and commenter IP addresses under the same circumstances that it uses and discloses personally-identifying information as described below, except that blog commenter IP addresses are visible and disclosed to the administrators of the blog where the comment was left.

**Gathering of Personally-Identifying Information**

Certain visitors to SearchWPâs websites choose to interact with SearchWP in ways that require SearchWP to gather personally-identifying information. The amount and type of information that SearchWP gathers depends on the nature of the interaction. For example, we ask visitors who comment on our blog to provide a username and email address. Those who wish to receive SearchWP updates via email, we collect their emails. In each case, SearchWP collects such information only insofar as is necessary or appropriate to fulfill the purpose of the visitorâs interaction with SearchWP. SearchWP does not disclose personally-identifying information other than as described below. And visitors can always refuse to supply personally-identifying information, with the caveat that it may prevent them from engaging in certain website-related activities.

**Aggregated Statistics**

SearchWP may collect statistics about the behavior of visitors to its websites. For instance, SearchWP may monitor the most popular pages on the SearchWP.com site or use spam screened by the Akismet service to help identify spam. SearchWP may display this information publicly or provide it to others. However, SearchWP does not disclose personally-identifying information other than as described below.

**Protection of Certain Personally-Identifying Information**

SearchWP discloses potentially personally-identifying and personally-identifying information only to those of its employees, contractors and affiliated organizations that (i) need to know that information in order to process it on SearchWPâs behalf or to provide services available at SearchWPâs websites, and (ii) that have agreed not to disclose it to others. Some of those employees, contractors and affiliated organizations may be located outside of your home country; by using SearchWPâs websites, you consent to the transfer of such information to them. SearchWP will not rent or sell potentially personally-identifying and personally-identifying information to anyone. Other than to its employees, contractors and affiliated organizations, as described above, SearchWP discloses potentially personally-identifying and personally-identifying information only in response to a subpoena, court order or other governmental request, or when SearchWP believes in good faith that disclosure is reasonably necessary to protect the property or rights of SearchWP, third parties or the public at large. If you are a registered user of an SearchWP website and have supplied your email address, SearchWP may occasionally send you an email to tell you about new features, solicit your feedback, or just keep you up to date with whatâs going on with SearchWP and our products. We primarily use our various product blogs to communicate this type of information, so we expect to keep this type of email to a minimum. If you send us a request (for example via a support email or via one of our feedback mechanisms), we reserve the right to publish it in order to help us clarify or respond to your request or to help us support other users. SearchWP takes all measures reasonably necessary to protect against the unauthorized access, use, alteration or destruction of potentially personally-identifying and personally-identifying information.

**Cookies**

A cookie is a string of information that a website stores on a visitorâs computer, and that the visitorâs browser provides to the website each time the visitor returns. SearchWP uses cookies to help SearchWP identify and track visitors, their usage of SearchWP website, and their website access preferences. SearchWP visitors who do not wish to have cookies placed on their computers should set their browsers to refuse cookies before using SearchWPâs websites, with the drawback that certain features of SearchWPâs websites may not function properly without the aid of cookies.

**Usage Tracking**

SearchWP may collect certain information through usage tracking. The purpose of usage tracking helps SearchWP to better understand our users and their website needs by looking at a range of variables in their SearchWP plugin, WordPress, and web host. This information allows us to continuously improve our product as well as our Q&A / testing process. The information may include the license key (and type), plugin activation dates, statistics to help us make sure our features and updates get tested in situations that closely match our customersâ site environments and SearchWP usage.

**Business Transfers**

If SearchWP, or substantially all of its assets were acquired, or in the unlikely event that SearchWP goes out of business or enters bankruptcy, user information would be one of the assets that is transferred or acquired by a third party. You acknowledge that such transfers may occur, and that any acquirer of SearchWP may continue to use your personal information as set forth in this policy.

**Ads**

Ads appearing on any of our websites may be delivered to users by advertising partners, who may set cookies. These cookies allow the ad server to recognize your computer each time they send you an online advertisement to compile information about you or others who use your computer. This information allows ad networks to, among other things, deliver targeted advertisements that they believe will be of most interest to you. This Privacy Policy covers the use of cookies by SearchWP and does not cover the use of cookies by any advertisers.

**Comments**

Comments and other content submitted to Akismet anti-spam service are saved on our servers unless they were marked as false positives, in which case we store them long enough to use them to improve the service to avoid future false positives.

**Privacy Policy Changes**

Although most changes are likely to be minor, SearchWP may change its Privacy Policy from time to time, and in SearchWPâs sole discretion. SearchWP encourages visitors to frequently check this page for any changes to its Privacy Policy. Your continued use of this site after any change in this Privacy Policy will constitute your acceptance of such change.

*Last updated: July 7, 2021*

---

### [Advanced Settings](https://searchwp.com/v3/docs/settings/advanced/)

**Published:** February 12, 2019
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

## Advanced Settings

The **Advanced** tab of the SearchWP settings screen allows you to configure SearchWP on a lower level, enabling various features that may be helpful to the search experience on your website.

There are four main areas of the Advanced tab of the SearchWP settings screen:

- [Stopwords](#stopwords)
- [Synonyms](#synonyms)
- [Actions & Settings](#actions-settings)
- [Export/Import](#export-import)

### Stopwords

[Stopwords](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/stopwords/) are terms that are intentionally excluded from both the search index and any submitted searches. These words are very common and often do not contribute to the relevance of results and as such are completely ignored. You have full control over the Stopwords used by SearchWP. [More info.](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/stopwords/)

[![Screenshot of Stopword management in SearchWP](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-settings-stopwords.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-settings-stopwords.png)

[More Info](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/stopwords/)

### Synonyms

SearchWP allows you to establish [synonyms](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/synonyms/) for search queries to allow for more relevant results. If you notice (by examining your statistics) that visitors are searching for âgreyâ but you spell it âgrayâ you can teach SearchWP what it should do in that case. [More info.](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/synonyms/)

[![Screenshot of synonym management in SearchWP](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-settings-synonyms-1030x624.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-settings-synonyms.png)

[More Info](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/synonyms/)

### Actions & Settings

Using the Action buttons you can reset SearchWPâs index, wake up the indexer if it appears to have stalled, and restore dismissed notices. There are individual settings to modify how SearchWP operates when indexing and searching. [More info.](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/actions-settings/)

[![Screenshot of Actions and Settings in SearchWP](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-settings-actions-settings.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-settings-actions-settings.png)

[More Info](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/actions-settings/)

### Import/Export

Using the Advanced settings screen you can easily export existing engine configurations and import them into another WordPress installation that has SearchWP. This can be beneficial when moving a site between domains, or even backing up your engine configuration over time.

[![Screenshot of SearchWP's import feature](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-settings-import.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-settings-import.png)

---

### [\SearchWP\Attribute](https://searchwp.com/documentation/classes/searchwp-attribute/)

**Published:** March 6, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

# `\SearchWP\Attribute`

Table of Contents

- [Arguments](#arguments)
- [Methods](#methods)
- [Hooks](#hooks)

`\SearchWP\Attribute` responsible for modeling the individual parts that make up a [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/). For example:

- Entry title
- Entry content
- Entry slug
- Custom Fields
- etcâ¦

## Arguments

When instantiating a new `\SearchWP\Attribute` an `array` of the following can be provided:

**`name` (`string`)**A unique name. *(default:* **`''`***)***`label` (`string`)**Display label. *(default: value of* **`name`***)***`default` (`false|integer`)**If this `\SearchWP\Attribute` should be enabled by default return `int`. *(default:* **`false`***)***`options` (`array|callable`)**When provided, multiple instances of this `\SearchWP\Attribute` are considered, e.g. Custom Fields each with a `meta_key`. *(default:* **`[]`***)***`allow_custom` (`boolean`)**Whether user-defined `option`s can be added. *(default:* **`false`***)***`phrases` (`boolean|array`)**Whether this `\SearchWP\Attribute` should support [phrase based searching](/documentation/knowledge-base/quoted-phrase-sentence-search-support/). *(default:* **`false`***)*When `array` the following array keys apply:`table` (`string`) The database table name.`column` (`string`) The database table column to match phrases against.`id` (`string`) The database table column tracking entry IDs.**`data` (`mixed`)**The data for this `\SearchWP\Attribute`. *(default:* **`null`***)*When `callable` the following arguments are passed:`id` (`string`) [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/) ID.`chosen_option` (`string`) The chosen `\SearchWP\Attribute` `option` when applicable.## Methods

When working with `\SearchWP\Attribute` there are a number of methods to consider.

**`get_name()`**Getter for name.**`get_label( $source )`**Getter for label. If a [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/) is provided the [`searchwp\source\attribute\label`](/documentation/hooks/searchwp-source-attribute-label/) filter is applied.**`get_phrases()`**Getter for phrase support argument value.**`options_static()`**Whether `options` is static (as opposed to `callable`).**`get_options()`**Getter for `options`.**`get_settings()`**Getter for settings (i.e. as considered by an [`\SearchWP\Engine`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-engine/)).**`get_default()`**Getter for `default`.**`set_settings()` `(integer|array $settings)`**Setter for settings (i.e. as considered by an [`\SearchWP\Engine`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-engine/)). Use `integer` when there are no `options`, else use `array` with keys for each option and corresponding `integer` values.**`get_data()` `( $id, $option = '', $raw = false )`**Getter for `data`.`$id` (`string`) [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/) ID.`$option` (`string`) `option` value, when applicable.`$raw` (`boolean`) Whether to return the raw data, default is to tokenize.## Hooks

There are a number of hooks available to further modify the behavior of `\SearchWP\Attribute`:

- [ `searchwp\source\attribute\label` ](https://searchwp.com/documentation/hooks/searchwp-source-attribute-label/)

---

### [Search](https://searchwp.com/documentation/search/)

**Published:** March 5, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

# Search

Table of Contents

- [How SearchWP Works](#how)
- [Indexing](#indexing)
- [Searching](#searching)
- [Document Processing](#document-processing)

## How SearchWP Works

On the most rudimentary level SearchWP does two things:

1. Indexes content it finds by analyzing your [Engines](/documentation/setup/engines/)
2. Accepts searches and performs them against its Index

There is a lot of nuance of both operations, but the core and fundamental purpose of SearchWP is to index content and make it searchable. SearchWP goes one step further by integrating itself into WordPressâ native search process. This allows SearchWP to be a âcode-lessâ solution in many cases.

SearchWP also aims to be a developerâs best friend not only by instantly improving native WordPress search results, but also facilitating custom built search implementations using any combination of [Engines](/documentation/setup/engines/) and [Queries](/documentation/search/searchwp-query/).

## Indexing

Before SearchWP can provide awesome search results, it needs to build itself a proper search Index. It does this by utilizing a set of custom database tables and a process called tokenization.

Once SearchWPâs Indexer has an [Engine](/documentation/setup/engines/) to work with, it will use that Engine configuration to scour your site for any content that needs to be indexed using its own background process. That means (in [most cases](https://searchwp.com/documentation/knowledge-base/alternate-indexer/)) you do not need to keep a browser window open in order for SearchWPâs Indexer to work.

SearchWPâs Engines are comprised of [Sources](/documentation/sources/), and each Source has its own set of [Attributes](/documentation/setup/engines/#attributes) and [Rules](/documentation/setup/engines/#rules). The Indexer takes this all into consideration when it finds content and retrieves what it needs from each applicable entry.

That content for each entry is then tokenzied i.e. broken up into small pieces that the search algorithm can work with. The Indexer cycles until all applicable entries have been processed.

The Indexer is configured to run as fast as possible (e.g. reduce the time it takes to build its index) without overloading the server and disrupting visitors.

Once the initial index has been built, the Indexer will monitor content edits on your site and apply very small delta updates to any entries that are added/removed/edited over time.

## Searching

With its Index built, SearchWP is able to query against it and provide relevant search results extremely fast. This is primarily due to the tokenization process accomplished by the Indexer.

There are three ways SearchWP performs searches:

1. Intercepting native WordPress search requests
2. Programmatically using [`\SearchWP\Query`](https://searchwp.com/documentation/classes/searchwp-query/)
3. Programmatically using [`SWP_Query`](https://searchwp.com/documentation/classes/swp_query/)

When a search is performed, an Engine is always applied. That Engine configuration determines which results are applicable and also influences the ranking of the results by taking into consideration the relevancy weights of each Source [Attribute](/documentation/setup/engines/#attributes).

Programmatic invocation of [`\SearchWP\Query`](https://searchwp.com/documentation/classes/searchwp-query/) and [`SWP_Query`](https://searchwp.com/documentation/classes/swp_query/) will require the setup of both a search form and search results template, which is covered in the [Supplemental Engine Guide](/documentation/search/supplemental-engine/).

## Document Processing

As part of the [Indexing](#indexing) process SearchWP will extract text content from supported documents when applicable. When this process is successful, the parsed content will be tokenized and processed by the Indexer as though it were stored as an [Attribute](/documentation/setup/engines/#attributes). Document content can receive its own relevance weight when Media has been added to an Engine.

For more information on how SearchWP handles Documents, please see this KB article: [Document Processing Details](https://searchwp.com/documentation/knowledge-base/customizing-and-verifying-document-content/)

---

### [\SearchWP\Entry](https://searchwp.com/documentation/classes/searchwp-entry/)

**Published:** March 6, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

# `\SearchWP\Entry`

Table of Contents

- [Arguments](#arguments)
- [Methods](#methods)
- [Hooks](#hooks)

`\SearchWP\Entry` is responsible for modeling an individual [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/) entry.

## Arguments

When instantiating a new `\SearchWP\Entry` the following arguments are considered:

**`source` (`string|\SearchWP\Source`)**Either the [`\SearchWP\Source` `name` or the ](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/)[`\SearchWP\Source` itself. *(required)*](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/)**`id` (`string`)**The database row ID of the [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/). *(required)***`get_data` (`boolean`)**Whether the data should be retrieved at instantiation. *(default:* **`true`***)***`all_attributes` (`boolean`)**Whether to retrieve all of the registered [`\SearchWP\Attribute`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-attribute/) data, or only those that are used in an [`\SearchWP\Engine`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-engine/) *(default:* **`false`***)*## Methods

When working with `\SearchWP\Entry` there are a number of methods to consider.

**`get_id()`**Getter for ID.**`get_source()`**Getter for [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/).**`update_data( $all_attributes = false )`**Updates data.**`get_data()`**Getter for data.**`native()`**Returns a version of this `\SearchWP\Entry` that the associated [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/) was modeled around e.g. for Posts a proper `WP_Post` will be returned, populated appropriately.**Note:** Not all [`\SearchWP\Attribute`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-attribute/)s will be populated for core [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/)s e.g. Custom Fields.## Hooks

There are a number of hooks available to further modify the behavior of `\SearchWP\Entry`:

- [ `searchwp\entry\native` ](https://searchwp.com/documentation/hooks/searchwp-entry-native/)
- [ `searchwp\entry\data` ](https://searchwp.com/documentation/hooks/searchwp-entry-data/)

---

### [Search Shortcode Content](https://searchwp.com/search-shortcode-content/)

**Published:** November 11, 2016
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

SearchWP was designed and built to make searching your WordPress site comprehensive. A popular way to save time and effort when it comes to adding new features to your site is to use Shortcodes.

[Shortcodes](https://codex.wordpress.org/shortcode) are essentially macros for WordPress. Many complex features can be added to your WordPress site with a single Shortcode. For instance, when adding an image gallery to a page on your site:

[![Screenshot of Inserting a Gallery into a Page](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-12.03-PM.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-12.03-PM.png)Inserting an image Gallery into a Page

There is a lot of important content included in this gallery! For example:

- Image filenames
- Image descriptions
- Image alt text

All of this is powered by an image gallery Shortcode, however. We can switch to the Text tab to see what that means:

[![Screenshot of an image gallery Shortcode](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-12.06-PM.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-12.06-PM.png)The Shortcode powering this image gallery, this is all native WordPress search uses!

WordPress native search doesnât see all of the content you added to your gallery, it only sees:

`[gallery ids="339,340,341,342,343,344,345"]`

SearchWP was built to process that Shortcode when it builds your index, allowing for all of that image gallery content to be searchable!

This functionality is not enabled by default (to protect against unwanted Shortcode expansion) but it can be enabled by ticking the checkbox to do so on the Advanced tab of the SearchWP settings screen and rebuilding the index.

---

### [Automatic "Did you mean" (search query corrections)](https://searchwp.com/v3/docs/settings/advanced/automatic-did-you-mean/)

**Published:** July 30, 2019
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

**As of version 3.1,** SearchWP supports âdid you mean?â-style search corrections when a search yields no results and SearchWP can determine what the visitor *likely* meant to search for.

To enable this functionality, tick the appropriate checkbox on the Advanced tab of the SearchWP settings screen:

[![Screenshot of SearchWP settings screen Advanced tab](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-did-you-mean-setting.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-did-you-mean-setting.png)

(Alternatively you can also enable this behavior using this hook: [`searchwp_do_suggestions`](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_do_suggestions/))

When this feature is enabled, SearchWP will *automatically* attempt to correct any searches that provide zero results **in addition to** outputting a notice above The Loop of your search results:

â ï¸ Style (CSS) customization is likely required â ï¸

[![Screenshot of SearchWP's "Did you mean?" automatic output](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-did-you-mean-initial.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-did-you-mean-initial.png)

If you would like to prevent the automatic output of this notice you can use the [`searchwp_auto_output_revised_search_query`](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_auto_output_revised_search_query/) hook to disable it. Subsequently you can use the [`searchwp_revised_search_query`](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_revised_search_query/) to implement your own in place of the automatic output.

The above screenshot outlines the automatic output in [Twenty Nineteen](https://wordpress.org/themes/twentynineteen/), a default WordPress theme. The following CSS can be added to improve its appearance:

**Note:** (You can add Custom CSS to your site by navigating to Appearance > Customize > Additional CSS in your WordPress Dashboard)

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       .searchwp-revised-search-notice {     margin: 0 calc(10% + 60px) 1em;     padding: 0.6em;     background: #f7f7f7;     border: 1px solid #e7e7e7;     border-radius: 2px;    }        .searchwp-suggested-revision-query {     font-style: italic;     font-weight: bold;    }  

 [view raw](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/764ce18dd0dba053d774c6b6aedf9f1f/raw/bc7bcfe954526853d2bfb642d49049d81a88e2d9/style.css) [ style.css ](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/764ce18dd0dba053d774c6b6aedf9f1f#file-style-css) hosted with â¤ by [GitHub](https://github.com) 

Which results in a notification that better matches the site design:

[![Screenshot of customized styling of SearchWP's "Did you mean?" automatic output](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-did-you-mean-styled.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-did-you-mean-styled.png)

You can override the default output by adding something like this to your themeâs `functions.php`:

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       <?php        // Override SearchWP's "Did you mean?" output.    class MySearchwpDidYouMean {     private $args;         function \_\_construct() {     // Prevent SearchWP's automatic "Did you mean?" output.     add\_filter( 'searchwp\_auto\_output\_revised\_search\_query', '\_\_return\_false' );         // Grab the "Did you mean?" arguments to use later.     add\_action( 'searchwp\_revised\_search\_query', function( $args ) {     $this->args = $args;     } );         // Output custom "Did you mean?" message at the top of The Loop.     add\_action( 'loop\_start', function() {     if ( empty( $this->args ) ) {     return '';     }         $phrase\_query = str\_replace( array( 'â', 'â' ), '"', SWP()->original\_query ); // Accommodate curly quotes.     echo '<p class="searchwp-revised-search-notice">';     echo wp\_kses(     sprintf(     // Translators: First placeholder is the quoted search string. Second placeholder is the search string without quotes.     \_\_( 'No results found for <em class="searchwp-revised-search-original">%s</em>. Showing results for <em class="searchwp-suggested-revision-query">%s</em>', 'searchwp' ),     esc\_html( $phrase\_query ),     esc\_html( str\_replace( '"', '', implode( ' ', $this->args\['terms'\] ) ) )     ),     array(     'em' => array(     'class' => array(),     ),     )     );     echo '</p>';     } );     }    }        new MySearchwpDidYouMean();  

 [view raw](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/3d46a9697b87c71b6d37963d1aa6d1fd/raw/92ee55975c33a442ad34b066b5224749dbb9d909/functions.php) [ functions.php ](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/3d46a9697b87c71b6d37963d1aa6d1fd#file-functions-php) hosted with â¤ by [GitHub](https://github.com) 

## Interoperability with partial matches

When âDid you mean?â functionality is enabled ***and*** [partial matching](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/partial-matches/) is enabled, SearchWP will first attempt to find exact partial matches and utilize partial match functionality.

If no partial matches are found, SearchWP will then utilize its âDid you mean?â functionality, determining the best search correction it can. If no search correction can be determined, SearchWP will show zero results.

### [Showcase](https://searchwp.com/showcase/)

**Published:** July 6, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

These sites were built by SearchWP customers. Please [submit yours](/contact) to have it listed here!

- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/showcase-ltk.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/showcase-ltk.png) 
    
    ### LTK
    
    âUse case: the normal WP search wasnât very good, so I checked out some different solutions (a couple I built myself) and then went with SearchWP.â
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-tokyo-spark.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-tokyo-spark.png) 
    
    ### Tokyo Spark
    
    **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    Originally I planned for Tokyo Spark to be a curation site where Iâd use quite a few automation tools to create a sort of collection of relevant content. This would have meant a lot of articlesâ¦ something like 20-30 new articles every day. I needed a better way to integrate a nice search into my site, the ability to build SearchWP into my templates was perfect. Plus I needed a way to allow a search that would only search especially through certain content while excluding others that would have returned irrelevant information.
    
    **How did you evaluate whether SearchWP solved the original problem?**
    
    I dug through the documentation and discovered the supplemental engine stuff. This led me to the template code which gave me the idea to use this as an integral part to help my readers find things on my site.
    
    **Did working with SearchWP open the possibility for additional features on your site you hadnât considered before?**
    
    Yes, the related content extension allowed me to add sections to my articles that link over to properly index-related content, rather than just using categories or manually linking to related articles. Plus I donât need to worry about performance concerns that other related-posts type plugins add.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-pillars-community-health.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-pillars-community-health.png) 
    
    ### Pillars Community Health
    
    **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    WPs search would not consistently output accurate results. Nor does it elegantly handle custom post types.
    
    **How did you evaluate whether SearchWP solved the original problem?**
    
    Sounds obvious, but SearchWP delivers relevant search results. And since custom fields are such a huge part of every site build, having the ability to include those in search results is awesome.
    
    **Did working with SearchWP open the possibility for additional features on your site you hadnât considered before?**
    
    Absolutely. Every one of my earlier sites uses Advanced Custom Fields extensively, so being able to add custom fields to the search results was huge. And your search highlight and WP Job Manager add-ons have been huge time savers. Not having to scour through the plugin repo to find decent 3rd party plugins to replicate these features was both time-saving and gave me peace of mind.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-free-together.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-free-together.png) 
    
    ### Free Together
    
    (site built by Bill Erickson)
    
    âAfter digging into SearchWP for a few projects, I found useful addons like Related, Metrics, and AJAX Live Search that could be used to improve even more sites. I now default to using SearchWP on all projects.â
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-wavs.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-wavs.png) 
    
    ### WavsGB
    
     **Note:** [FacetWP](https://facetwp.com/?ref=1) is used for filtering, *not* SearchWP **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    We wanted visitors to be able to search taxonomyâs and custom field data as well as standard post titles. Customers are searching for a specific types of vehicle and we wanted them to be able to search make and model as well as with keywords like winch, lift, sliding door etc..
    
    **What steps did you take to integrate SearchWP?**
    
    Itâs a simple install â just a standard install with some css styling and results page template.
    
    **How did you evaluate whether SearchWP solved the original problem?**
    
    SearchWP was installed by a previous client so I knew how well it worked and when WavsGB wanted a good reliable search solution I knew SearchWP would do the job! The on-site documentation was also helpful and also knowing which extensions are available let me know exactly what I could achieve. I also backed up my own experience by reading online articles/reviews which confirmed it was the right solution.
    
    **Did working with SearchWP open the possibility for additional features on your site you hadnât considered before?**
    
    Nothing technical with WavsGB â¦yet, but the site is evolving so it maybe that we add extra features or utilise it in a new way at some point.
    However the search statistics are proving invaluable â it is informing the vehicle buyersâ decisions as to which models and features are being searched for by potential customers and to try and accommodate when they buy new vehicle stock.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-nutrition-advisory-group.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-nutrition-advisory-group.png) 
    
    ### AZA Nutrition Advisory Group
    
    **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    Our website search results werenât very good with default WordPress install. Our site publishes a lot of documents, and default WP does not index/search the document contents. Also, it didnât always show what we would consider the best results first.
    
    **How did you evaluate whether SearchWP solved the original problem?**
    
    At first, our search didnât improve. Our theme conflicted with SearchWP somehow. We contacted the developer and got a very speedy response that cleared up the issue. Now our results are exactly what we were hoping for.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-paladin.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-paladin.png) 
    
    ### Paladin Data Corporation
    
    **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    We needed a way to have multiple searches, each with their own results page. Needed a better fuzzy search.
     
    **What steps did you take to integrate SearchWP?**
    
    I wrote a couple supporting plugins that work with searchwp to display different searches based on user role, and display custom results pages for each of our search engines.
     
    **How did you evaluate whether SearchWP solved the original problem?**
    
    Could we accomplish multiple search engines based on category and tag criteria, and display based on user role, as well as the ability to manually influence search results.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-studyfinds.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-studyfinds.png) 
    
    ### StudyFinds
    
    Site built by Bill Erickson, note from developer:
    
    âThe key problem was WordPress search just didnât work. Sites with lots of content had trouble exposing relevant content in searches, and more custom sites that utilized a lot of meta / taxonomies werenât being indexed.â
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/scrrenshot-showcase-viking-electronics.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/scrrenshot-showcase-viking-electronics.png) 
    
    ### Viking Electronics
    
    **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    Vikingâs products have cryptic, punctuated alpha-numeric names. Names like âAES-2000â, âFXI-1Aâ, âK-1700â, etcâ¦ as well as dozens of extremely similarly named product variants that have differentiators as part of the name, like âK-1700-IP-EWPâ. While many site visitors will search for english phrases (ex: âweather resistant phoneâ) many of the siteâs visitors are existing customers there for support, and type in a product name or partial product name to make their visit as efficient as possible. WordPressâs native search was essentially useless for this kind of query.
    
    **What steps did you take to integrate SearchWP? (e.g. just install, activate, and save settings vs. some custom coding)**
    
    Custom code was involved. Due to the clientâs need for reliable Product name query results and the afore-mentioned difficulty of those names themselves, the SWP support team helped me with a few regex solutions to turn the product names into indexable search phrases â not only for exact query matches but for variants (such as querying the product name without any hyphens). Of course looking at the Search Stats (now âMetricsâ) was extremely helpful in figuring out how users were using search. Without stats, it would have been a guessing game at best. Customizations included: Ignoring logged-in usersâ searches, separating results based on post-type, weighting exact matches higher than partials, removing pagination for search results, and the regex modifications for indexing product names.
    
    **How did you evaluate whether SearchWP solved the original problem?**
    
    It was immediately obvious that the improvement was night and day. Native WP search was throwing darts at a wall in the dark and not even hitting the wallâ¦maybe not even picking up the dart. The SWP results were immediately significantly better, and became even more useful and reliable for site visitors once the extensions and regex modifications were implemented (with the help of SWP support staff and documentation).
    
    **Did working with SearchWP open the possibility for additional features on your site you hadnât considered before?**
    
    Yes. I also implemented some non-SWP search functionality that piggybacks on the main query field. Many users go to the site specifically for documentation that is known as a âDODâ (Document On Demand) that are named as three digit numbers. The user can enter their DOD number in the search field, and if it is valid they are given a different result treatmentâ¦all while not interfering with the main siteâs search (in the case of a positive query result that coincides with a DOD #).
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-maine-highlands.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-maine-highlands.png) 
    
    ### The Maine Highlands
    
    **How did you evaluate whether SearchWP solved the original problem?**
    
    Well it was easy because you totally blew competitors out of the water by how easy it was to simply install, activate, and index the site. Not to mention the weight multipliers was super easy to use because of the simple UI with the sliders. Love that!
    
    Also, biggest thing for my clients was that you provide a widget on the main dashboard page and also a separate page which has a beautiful chart/graph and nice little widgets showing today, week, month, year. Super great for clients to see these all broken down as such.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-greenville-maine.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-greenville-maine.png) 
    
    ### Town of Greenville Maine
    
    **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    It was important for some of my sites to see what users were searching for so they could adjust links or make things like pdfs easier to access. Specifically the Town of Greenville.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-tigunia.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-tigunia.png) 
    
    ### Tigunia
    
    Site built by Bill Erickson, note from developer:
    
    âI use SearchWP on **every** website that has on-site search. If thereâs a search form in the design files, Iâm using SearchWP. Thereâs no reason not to make the search results more relevant to visitors.â
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-great-lakes-center.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-great-lakes-center.png) 
    
    ### Great Lakes Center
    
    **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    WPs search would not consistently output accurate results. Nor does it elegantly handle custom post types.
    
    **How did you evaluate whether SearchWP solved the original problem?**
    
    Sounds obvious, but SearchWP delivers relevant search results. And since custom fields are such a huge part of every site build, having the ability to include those in search results is awesome.
    
    **Did working with SearchWP open the possibility for additional features on your site you hadnât considered before?**
    
    Absolutely. Every one of my earlier sites uses Advanced Custom Fields extensively, so being able to add custom fields to the search results was huge. And your search highlight and WP Job Manager add-ons have been huge time savers. Not having to scour through the plugin repo to find decent 3rd party plugins to replicate these features was both time-saving and gave me peace of mind.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-barn2.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-barn2.png) 
    
    ### Barn2 Plugins
    
    **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    We use the Heroic Knowledge Base plugin to power the documentation for our WordPress and WooCommerce plugins, which consists of over 300 articles. As our user base and knowledge base grew, we needed a more advanced search â both at the top of every page in the knowledge base, and on our support request form which is powered by Gravity Forms and requires users to search the knowledge base before they can submit a request.
    
    The problems with the previous search were:
    
    â The results were not very relevant. For example, if you searched for âlicense keyâ then our article about license key problems wouldnât even be in the top 10 results! 
    â The keyword search looked within our entire knowledge base. As a result, customers were often shown results relating to plugins that they were not using. 
    â It was impossible to get information on the exact keywords people were searching for, failed searches, etc.
    
    I reported these limitations to Hero Themes, who recommended that we combine their knowledge base plugin with SearchWP. One of our developers spent some time integrating the two, and the results were a vast improvement.
    
    **Did working with SearchWP open the possibility for additional features on your site you hadnât considered before?**
    
    Absolutely â now we use SearchWP, we can make ongoing improvements to the knowledge base search based on data collected from our site. For example, after implementing SearchWP, it occurred to me that it would be good if we could include tutorials (which are blog posts and not knowledge base articles) in the knowledge base search results. It turned out that this was fairly straightforward because of SearchWP. We just needed to include all posts tagged with âTutorialâ in the search query.
    
    SearchWP also provides valuable information that enables us to improve the content of our knowledge base. For example, I have set a reminder for myself to check the data on failed searches once a month. A few weeks before WordPress 5.0 was released with the new Gutenberg editor, I discovered that lots of people had been searching for the word âGutenbergâ in our knowledge base. I typed âGutenbergâ into the search and it didnât appear in any of our articles! This showed that there was a definite need to publish a dedicated article about how our plugins work with Gutenberg, as well as referring to Gutenberg in context within the existing articles. Thanks to SearchWP, anyone searching our knowledge base for Gutenberg will instantly find the information they need.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-ladder-up.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-ladder-up.png) 
    
    ### Ladder Up
    
    **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    WPs search would not consistently output accurate results. Nor does it elegantly handle custom post types.
    
    **How did you evaluate whether SearchWP solved the original problem?**
    
    Sounds obvious, but SearchWP delivers relevant search results. And since custom fields are such a huge part of every site build, having the ability to include those in search results is awesome.
    
    **Did working with SearchWP open the possibility for additional features on your site you hadnât considered before?**
    
    Absolutely. Every one of my earlier sites uses Advanced Custom Fields extensively, so being able to add custom fields to the search results was huge. And your search highlight and WP Job Manager add-ons have been huge time savers. Not having to scour through the plugin repo to find decent 3rd party plugins to replicate these features was both time-saving and gave me peace of mind.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-bill-erickson.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-bill-erickson.png) 
    
    ### Bill Erickson
    
    The key problem was WordPress search just didnât work. Sites with lots of content had trouble exposing relevant content in searches, and more custom sites that utilized a lot of meta / taxonomies werenât being indexed.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-lafebervet.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-lafebervet.png) 
    
    ### Lafeber Vet
    
     **Note:** [FacetWP](https://facetwp.com/?ref=1) is used for filtering, *not* SearchWP **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    The posts on Vet are highly categorized, have several taxonomies and tagged. We also use custom fields pretty extensively. Regular search was just not cutting it and was too generic. Being able to give weight to the taxonomies and fields made a huge difference.
    
    **How did you evaluate whether SearchWP solved the original problem?**
    
    The Vet site is all about exotic species. My editor complained that when she searched for âcrowâ, she got a bunch of posts with words like âcrowdingâ. When we implemented this, âcrowâ now just brings up one post, the one on the corvidae family, just what she wanted.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-bellaireprobate.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-bellaireprobate.png) 
    
    ### Russel W. Hall & Associates, P.C.
    
    Site built by Bill Erickson, quote from developer:
    
    âMost sites are just install, activate, and save default settings. Sometimes weâll add taxonomies to be indexed, or add/remove CPTs from the search engine. Sometimes weâll build supplemental search engines. But it rarely takes more than a few minutes to setup.â
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-aerospace-america.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-aerospace-america.png) 
    
    ### Aerospace America
    
    âUse case: the normal WP search wasnât very good, so I checked out some different solutions (a couple I built myself) and then went with SearchWP.â
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-wavelength.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-wavelength.png) 
    
    ### Wavelength Electronics
    
    **What problem came up that caused the need for a better search solution?**
    
    We were completely redesigning our website to modernize it and comply with some new Google requirements. At the time Google Search was moving to serving ads on sites using their search which was wholly unacceptable to us, so we asked our website consultant to choose a different search engine for our site.
    
    **How did you evaluate whether SearchWP solved the original problem?**
    
    As part of acceptance testing for the new website, we ran a number of search requests and studied the results. Initially the results were not prioritized in the best way for our business, so I requested that Product pages get highest priority in the results, which the contractor was able to implement using SearchWP.
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/sscreenshot-showcase-solutionsiq.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/sscreenshot-showcase-solutionsiq.png) 
    
    ### Solutions IQ, Accenture
    
    **Note:** Filtration was built by site developer and is *not* faciliated by SearchWP.
    
    Site built by Bill Erickson, note from developer:
    
    âOur evaluation process is simply having the client do searches on their old production site and our new development site and seeing the difference. The results are always better, and the clients love knowing they can tweak the ranking factors if they arenât getting the results they want.â
- [ ![Screenshot](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-postos.png) ](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/screenshot-showcase-postos.png) 
    
    ### PostOS
    
    **Note:** Filtration was built by site developer and is *not* faciliated by SearchWP.
    
    Site built by Bill Erickson, note from developer:
    
    âPostOS uses metaboxes and taxonomies for all content â the *post\_content* field is empty. SearchWP was instrumental in us building this project. They had a 20 year old web application that worked okay for entering / displaying the data, but they had no way of searching it.â

### [Search Custom Fields and Taxonomy Terms](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-custom-fields-meta-taxonomy-terms/)

**Published:** May 19, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

Native WordPress Search will only consider entry Title, Content, and Excerpt when it finds results. Limiting search results to those three attributes means that your site visitors are likely not finding the content theyâre looking for.

With plugins like [Advanced Custom Fields](https://www.advancedcustomfields.com/) having well over **1 million active installs** itâs no secret that a lot of valuable search terms are being ignored by WordPress native search.

SearchWP integrates with any plugin that stores data using Custom Fields (post meta) including:

- [Advanced Custom Fields](https://wordpress.org/plugins/advanced-custom-fields/) (free and [Pro](https://www.advancedcustomfields.com/pro/))
- [CMB2](https://wordpress.org/plugins/cmb2/)
- [Meta Box](https://metabox.io/) and [MB Custom Table](https://metabox.io/plugins/mb-custom-table/) data (see [Meta Box Integration](https://searchwp.com/extensions/meta-box-integration/))
- [Custom Field Suite](https://wordpress.org/plugins/custom-field-suite/)
- [Piklist](https://wordpress.org/plugins/piklist/)
- [Types](https://wordpress.org/plugins/types/)
- ***and many more (probably the one youâre using)!***

Taxonomy terms (Categories, Tags) also make *excellent* search keywords and can likely be considered to have more search relevance than content keywords because terms are meant to represent the entry as a whole.

 [![Screenshot of Source Attribute management](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-settings-engine-source-attributes.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-settings-engine-source-attributes.png) 

Pick and choose which Attributes to consider for each Source

SearchWP considers Custom Fields and Taxonomy Terms when performing searches. You can pick/choose which Custom Fields and Taxonomies make sense to search, and give each its own relevance weight.

 [![Screenshot highlighting weight adjustment](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-settings-engine-source-attributes-weights.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-settings-engine-source-attributes-weights.png) 

Assign relevance to individual Attributes

Note that SearchWP works like you do: thereâs an option to use âAny Custom Fieldâ as a single attribute, allowing you to consider content entered in any custom field for that entry. You can also opt to give more weight to Tags over Categories if your Tags usually have more pointed terms than Categories which are usually a bit more generic.

### Find out more

There is much more to explain about *how* SearchWP finds more relevant results than native WordPress search. Here is some additional documentation to check out:

- [Advanced Custom Fields and SearchWP](https://searchwp.com/advanced-custom-fields-acf/)
- [Using a Custom Field to Prioritize Search Results](https://searchwp.com/documentation/knowledge-base/custom-field-prioritize-results/)
- [Knowledge Base](https://searchwp.com/documentation/knowledge-base/)

---

### [Searching Ecommerce Product Details](https://searchwp.com/search-ecommerce-product-details-sku/)

**Published:** May 19, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

WordPress examines only Title, Content, and Excerpt when performing searches. Some ecommerce plugins do what they can to get around this issue by including some additional content when possible, but that quickly turns into unwieldy database queries that are slow and still donât return [results sorted by relevance](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-results-sorted-by-relevance/).

SearchWP properly [considers Custom Fields (metadata) and Taxonomy Terms](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-custom-fields-meta-taxonomy-terms/) when searching, which includes all of the product and course details you so painstakingly populated.

If your customers donât find what theyâre looking for, **theyâll move on**

Itâs vital that your online storefront produce accurate and comprehensive search results. Ecommerce is a very competitive space, and having a powerful on-site search experience will set you apart from everyone else.

## WordPress ignores product details

Take for example this sample online storefront:

**Note:** this example highlights [WooCommerce Integration](https://searchwp.com/extensions/woocommerce-integration/) which is a **Pro Extension**

 [![Screenshot of WooCommerce store](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-woocommerce.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-woocommerce.png) 

It looks like there's a lot of hats for sale here

There are hats in multiple places, right? Itâs a fan favorite so people must visit this site to buy hats. Letâs search for hat and see what else theyâve got for hats:

 [![Screenshot of empty search results](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-woocommerce-no-hats.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-woocommerce-no-hats.png) 

No search results for 'hats'

**Not one result!** It turns out in the fields that are searched by default, these products are called âbeaniesâ and âcapsâ not âhatâ which causes no results to show up, despite the word âhatâ appearing multiple times in areas that are not searched:

 [![Screenshot of hat appearing in product details](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-woocommerce-cap-2.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-woocommerce-cap-2.png) 

This content is ignored by default!

Using SearchWP this isnât an issue because [Custom Fields and Taxonomy Terms](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-custom-fields-meta-taxonomy-terms/) are considered for searching, which is very important for product details as outlined here!

When SearchWP and the [WooCommerce Integration Extension](https://searchwp.com/extensions/woocommerce-integration/) (**note:** this is a Pro Extension) are activated, SearchWP **automatically finds more and better results**!

 [![Screenshot of SearchWP and WooCommerce finding results](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-woocommerce-hat.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-woocommerce-hat.png) 

Finding caps and beanies when searching for hats

When configuring SearchWP you can pick and choose what content to make searchable, including Custom Fields, Taxonomies, and Comments which WooCommerce uses for Reviews. All of this content is supremely valuable when searching your online store, and leaving this content out means customers arenât finding what theyâre looking for.

Note that the example store above includes some Layered Navigation bits and also WooCommerceâs functionality to sort results by various criteria.

**SearchWP supports Layered Navigation and custom sorting** within WooCommerce. Customers will continue to be able to refine their search results using Layered Navigation Widgets, and using the sorting dropdown will override SearchWPâs default relevance-based sorting and respect the chosen sorting option.

### Gain new insight into your customers

Beyond providing comprehensive search results, paying attention to what your customers are searching for is an important duty of any store owner. Customer searches are direct insight into what your customers are seeking.

SearchWP provides extensive [Metrics](https://searchwp.com/search-metrics/) that give you insight into what can be improved on your site (including searches that yield no results) so as to optimize and drive more conversions.

Knowing what customers are searching for, what theyâre finding, **and what theyâre not finding** will give you a strong advantage over your competition and help you optimize your conversion rate.

### Find out more

To find out more about how SearchWP fixes WordPress search, have a look at this documentation:

- [SearchWP WooCommerce Integration Extension](https://searchwp.com/extensions/woocommerce-integration/)
- [Search Results Sorted by Relevance](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-results-sorted-by-relevance/)
- [Advanced Custom Fields and SearchWP](https://searchwp.com/advanced-custom-fields-acf/)
- [Knowledge Base](https://searchwp.com/documentation/knowledge-base/)
