### [FAQ](https://searchwp.com/documentation/faq/)

**Published:** May 21, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

---

### [Gain Visitor Insights from Search](https://searchwp.com/gain-visitor-insights-search/)

**Published:** November 11, 2016
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

SearchWP aims to help utilize search in many ways beyond improving your search results. SearchWP aims to take advantage of the fact that your site visitors will be providing you exclusive insight into what theyâre looking for.

When your visitors submit a search, SearchWP logs that search string and tracks searches over time. This allows you to gain insight into what people are looking for on your site:

[![Screenshot of SearchWP statistics](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-12.41-PM.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-12.41-PM.png)SearchWP logs searches over time, per search engine

***Important:*** SearchWP also logs any failed searches. This allows you to clearly see the difference between the words youâre using and the words your visitors are searching for!

Searches are logged per search engine, so you can keep up on exactly what your visitors are looking to find!

---

### [Searching PDF, Office, and Text Documents](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-pdf-office-text-documents/)

**Published:** May 20, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

When finding the most relevant search results, itâs important to consider data beyond the Title, Content, and Excerpt which is the only thing native WordPress includes.

In addition to searching [Custom Fields and Taxonomy Terms](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-custom-fields-meta-taxonomy-terms/) (and [providing results based on relevancy](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-results-sorted-by-relevance/)) SearchWP also takes into consideration the Media youâve uploaded to your site.

Many times the images uploaded to your entries include `alt` text, filenames with valuable keywords, and more for example.

SearchWP will also **parse your documents and make that content searchable** as well! Supported document types include PDFs, Office documents, plain (and rich) text, and more!

WordPress is able to display results from your Media library in the same way it displays results from any other post type, and SearchWP is able to find documents added to your Media library!

## Adding document content to search results

Adding document content to SearchWP is a matter of adding the *Document Content* attribute to Media within a SearchWP Engine:

 [![Screenshot of Media Attributes in SearchWP](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-document-content-1.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-document-content-1.png) 

With that in place you can set the relevancy weight to Document Content and SearchWP will extract content from documents within your Media library and index that content along with everything else thatâs been added to the Engine.

SearchWP also allows you to quickly restrict Media results based on file type by taking advantage of Rules:

 [![Screenshot of Media Rules](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-document-content-rules.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-document-content-rules.png) 

### How do Media results appear?

SearchWP *does not modify the way your theme displays search results* in any way.

Media (as far as WordPress and SearchWP is concerned) is just another post type entry that has a permalink, Title, and other details that your search results include.

SearchWPâs job is to find those Media results and include them when applicable, your theme will display a Media result in the same way it does any other post type result.

### Find out more

To learn more about how SearchWP fixes WordPress search, have a look at the following:

- [Search Results Sorted by Relevance](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-results-sorted-by-relevance/)
- [Setting up SearchWP Engines](https://searchwp.com/documentation/setup/engines/)
- [How SearchWP works](https://searchwp.com/documentation/search/)
- [Search forms and results for native WordPress searches](https://searchwp.com/documentation/search/native-search/)

---

### [Advanced Custom Fields and SearchWP](https://searchwp.com/advanced-custom-fields-acf/)

**Published:** May 19, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

SearchWP [considers Custom Fields](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-custom-fields-meta-taxonomy-terms/) just as much as it does Titles and Content. One of the most popular ways for developers to work with Custom Fields is using [Advanced Custom Fields](https://www.advancedcustomfields.com/). With **over 1 million active installations** ACF has become a primary go-to for many WordPress sites.

[![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/advanced-custom-fields.png)](https://www.advancedcustomfields.com/)

Because ACF is such a popular plugin SearchWP has some built in niceties when working with Advanced Custom Fields Field Groups.

## Search all of your ACF content

SearchWP allows you to add as many (or as few) Custom Fields to your search engines on a per-source, per-Engine basis. This allows you to customize (or generalize) your Engine(s) based on how youâve built your site. In many cases certain Custom Fields contain content thatâs given more focus on the site and therefore should be more relevant when performing a search.

SearchWPâs configuration and weighting system allows you to make as much of your Custom Field data searchable as youâd like. All you need to do is add your Fieldâs name to the Engine and youâre good to go!

 [![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-custom-fields-acf-repeatables-1030x586.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-custom-fields-acf-repeatables.png) 

You can pick and choose from any existing ACF Field and SearchWP has another level of support for ACF built right in:

### Direct support for ACF ârepeatablesâ

Advanced Custom Fields has a number of field types that facilitate grouping/replicating fields when editing content. Many times youâll want to group these ârepeatableâ fields within your SearchWP engine configuration. Doing so will allow you to tell SearchWP to include all ACF sub-fields as a single Attribute in your engine.

Advanced Custom Fields is a great solution to facilitate working with custom data structures in WordPress. Making that content searchable is very important and SearchWP can help make that happen!

### Find out more

There is much more to explain about *how* SearchWP finds more relevant results than native WordPress search. Here is some additional documentation to check out:

- [Search Custom Fields (meta) and Taxonomy Terms](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-custom-fields-meta-taxonomy-terms/)
- [Setting up SearchWP Engines](https://searchwp.com/documentation/setup/engines/)
- [How SearchWP works](https://searchwp.com/documentation/search/)
- [Search forms and results for native WordPress searches](https://searchwp.com/documentation/search/native-search/)
- [Knowledge Base](https://searchwp.com/documentation/knowledge-base/)

---

### [Search Results Sorted by Relevance](https://searchwp.com/wordpress-search-results-sorted-by-relevance/)

**Published:** May 19, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

SearchWP (by default) will return results based on their overall relevance. In fact, youâre able to pick and choose what content the search engine should consider *and* how important it is.

This helps to produce better results when compared to results which are sorted by publish date based on a limited keyword match.

Compare that to WordPress search which only considers the following:

- Title
- Content
- Excerpt

Native WordPress search results consist of any post type (that has not been excluded from search) with `LIKE` matches on the above fields, sorted primarily by Title matches and secondarily by post date in descending order.

More often than not this results in relevant content either not being returned at all (because the data is stored in a Custom Field or Taxonomy) or obscured by results that rank higher solely because of a more recent publish date.

## The problem

If your website is a business that is selling a service, itâs very likely that you have a few landing pages that are responsible for conversions. Pages you *want* your visitors to see.

Itâs also likely that you have a blog on your site thatâs constantly referencing the service you provide.

When it comes to native WordPress search, all of those blog posts are going to show up as the first results when in fact youâd prefer that the actual service page show up first because itâs the most relevant.

**SearchWP solves this problem by sorting results by relevance!**

### Relevance produces better search results

SearchWP was designed and built with the knowledge that WordPressâ extensibility is its strong suit, and valuable search data is stored in places other than the Title, Content, or Excerpt fields.

Taxonomy Terms (e.g. Categories, Tags, and custom Taxonomies) make for **excellent** search keywords, but native WordPress search doesnât consider them. With plugins like [Advanced Custom Fields](https://www.advancedcustomfields.com/) even primary content may not appear in the Content field.

SearchWP makes all content attributes first party considerations when performing searches (if you want them to be) including all Custom Fields and Taxonomies.

 [![Screenshot of SearchWP's Engine settings](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-settings-engines.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-settings-engines.png) 

Specify exactly what to consider when performing searches

When configuring SearchWP youâre able to pick and choose which sources youâd like to include in results. For each source you can determine not only which attributes to consider (e.g. Title, Content, Slug, Excerpt, Custom Fields, Taxonomies, Comments) you can also **define how relevant each attribute is compared to the others**.

Not only can you determine the relevancy weight for each attribute, you can have certain post types get more overall relevancy weight than others which will cause them to âbubble upâ to the top of results.

In the earlier example of a service provider having blog posts show up above the desired service page, you could give Post attributes less weight when compared to Pages, ensuring that your service page will raise its own ranking because you have determined that Pages have more relevance than Posts.

Using this relevancy weighting system you can specify that Titles and Taxonomies carry the most weight whereas the content may carry less because search keywords are likely to show up multiple times and be less unique.

### Find out more

There is much more to explain about *how* SearchWP finds more relevant results than native WordPress search. Here is some additional documentation to check out:

- [Setting up SearchWP Engines](https://searchwp.com/documentation/setup/engines/)
- [How SearchWP works](https://searchwp.com/documentation/search/)
- [Search forms and results for native WordPress searches](https://searchwp.com/documentation/search/native-search/)
- [Knowledge Base](https://searchwp.com/documentation/knowledge-base/)

---

### [Native Search](https://searchwp.com/documentation/search/native-search/)

**Published:** March 5, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

# Native Search

Table of Contents

- [Search forms and results templates](#forms-results-templates)
- [Extra URL query parameters](#url-query-parameters)
- [Is it working?](#is-it-working)

By default SearchWP will intercept native WordPress search requests and override native search results with its own.

## Search forms and results templates

When it comes to *native* WordPress search integration with SearchWP, **thereâs nothing to do!** WordPressâ default search forms provided by [`get_search_form()`](https://developer.wordpress.org/reference/functions/get_search_form/) (which youâre probably already using) are already set up to trigger a native WordPress search, which SearchWP intercepts.

If your search form has been customized, you can remove those customizations which will restore the form to making a `GET` request to the `site_url()` with a single query parameter `s` which is precisely what triggers a native WordPress search.

In the same way, you *also* do not need to customize your existing search results template! SearchWP does not intercept *the display* of search results for native WordPress searches, it only provides a new results set for the theme to display.

That said, SearchWP **does not facilitate search results display customization** as that is the responsibility of the theme. If you are looking to improve your native WordPress search results in such a way that SearchWP provides but is not compatible with WordPress (e.g. including [Users](/documentation/sources/users/) or another unsupported Source into your search results) you will need to use a [Supplemental Engine](/documentation/search/supplemental-engine/).

This outcome not a shortcoming of SearchWP or WordPress for that matter, but instead a recognition that WordPress core (and any related code, hooks, etc) **assumes** native search results are `WP_Post` objects. No problem! This is why we have [Supplemental Engines](/documentation/search/supplemental-engine/). ?

## Extra URL query parameters

SearchWP does not listen for all URL query parameters that may work with native WordPress searches. SearchWP expects a plain, boring URL similar to `https://example.com/?s=coffee+mug` â with no extra bells and or whistles, just the `?s` query parameter that triggers a native WordPress search.

If your implementation *does* want to support URL query parameters, thereâs a [Hook](/documentation/hooks/) for that!

## Is it working?

The best way to determine whether the results youâre seeing are provided by SearchWP would be to first [enable Debugging](/documentation/debugging/). Once enabled, SearchWP will output an HTML comment block on your search results pages. This comment block includes details about the search(es) run and the results found for each.

If the results youâre seeing donât match what SearchWP found, it means that SearchWPâs results were overridden by some other code. Not an issue! You can [open a support ticket](/documentation/setup/support-license/) and weâll help you find the culprit.

---

### [\SearchWP\Engine](https://searchwp.com/documentation/classes/searchwp-engine/)

**Published:** March 10, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

# `\SearchWP\Engine`

**Note:** SearchWP Engines are designed to be *fully* managed by the [settings screen](/documentation/setup/engines/).

Direct manipulation of an Engine should be done only when directed by Support.

Table of Contents

- [Arguments](#arguments)
- [Properties](#properties)
- [Methods](#methods)

`\SearchWP\Engine`s control the parameters used when performing a [`\SearchWP\Query`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-query/).

âï¸**Important**âï¸ `\SearchWP\Engine`s *fully* define what content is stored in SearchWPâs Index. As a result, `\SearchWP\Engine` management should take place on the [settings screen](/documentation/setup/engines/). There are *limited* modifications that can happen at runtime, outlined below.

## Arguments

When instantiating a new `\SearchWP\Engine` the following parameters can be passed:

**`name` (`string`)**The Engine name. *(default:* **`'default'`***)***`settings` (`array`)**Settings. *(default:* **`[]`***)*When the `$name` is that of a saved `\SearchWP\Engine` itâs settings will be loaded automatically.

When the `$settings` is empty, a default collections of settings will be used, based on the [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/) and [`\SearchWP\Attribute`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-attribute/) configurations available.

`$settings` cannot be directly manipulated programmatically as many changes to the `$settings` require that the Index be manipulated in some way. SearchWP needs to know about those changes to ensure accuracy of results. That is why `\SearchWP\Engine` updates must take place on the [settings screen](/documentation/setup/engines/) unless otherwise directed by Support.

## Properties

There are no public properties in `\SearchWP\Engine`.

## Methods

When working with `\SearchWP\Engine` there are a select few methods to consider.

**`remove_source( string $source_name )`**Excludes a [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/) for this request, does not drop anything from the Index.**`get_settings`**Getter for settings.**`get_sources`**Getter for [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/)s added to this Engine.**`get_source( string $source_name )`**Getter for a single [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/) added to this Engine.**`get_label`**Getter for label.**`get_name`**Getter for name.

---

### [\SearchWP\Index\Controller](https://searchwp.com/documentation/classes/searchwp-index-controller/)

**Published:** March 5, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

# `\SearchWP\Index\Controller`

**Note:** SearchWPâs Index was designed in such a way that all operations are handled *internally* without outside intervention.

Use of this class should be avoided unless directly guided by Support.

Table of Contents

- [Arguments](#arguments)
- [Properties](#properties)
- [Methods](#methods)
- [Hooks](#hooks)

`\SearchWP\Index\Controller` performs direct operations on the search Index. It is also responsible for maintaining delta update operations.

## Arguments

There are no arguments when instantiating a new `\SearchWP\Index\Controller`.

## Properties

There are no public properties in `\SearchWP\Index\Controller`.

## Methods

When working with `\SearchWP\Index\Controller` there are a number of methods to consider.

**`reset( $all_sites = false )`**Reset the Index by dropping all queues and data.`$all_sites` Whether to reset all sites. *(default:* **`false`** *only current site)***`get_alias`**Getter for Index alias to use in SQL queries.**`get_tables`**Getter for Index tables.**`get_sources`**Getter for all registered [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/)s.**`get_default_sources`**Getter for default [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/)s.**`get_source_by_name( string $name )`**Getter for registered [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/).`$name` Source name. *(required)***`get_entry_status( Entry $entry )`**Getter for [`\SearchWP\Entry`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-entry/) status.`$entry` Entry to check.**`get_source_id_status( string $source, $id )`**Getter for [`\SearchWP\Source`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-source/) status by ID.`$source` Source name.`$id` Source entry ID (required).**`trigger`**Trigger the delta update process.**`add ( Entry $entry )`**Add an [`\SearchWP\Entry`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-entry/) to the Index.**`drop( Source $source, $id, $force = false )`**Drops an [`\SearchWP\Entry`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-entry/) from the Index.`$source` Source.`$id` ID of the Entry to drop.`$force` Whether to drop immediately as opposed to allowing the background process to perform the task.**`get_stats`**Retrieve the stats for the Index right now.**`get_tokens_for_entry( Entry $entry )`**Retrieve the tokens for a single [`\SearchWP\Entry`](/documentation/classes/searchwp-entry/).## Hooks

There are a number of hooks available to further modify the behavior of `\SearchWP\Index\Controller`:

- [ `searchwp\background_process\http_basic_auth_credentials` ](https://searchwp.com/documentation/hooks/searchwp-background_process-http_basic_auth_credentials/)
- [ `searchwp\index\tokens_max` ](https://searchwp.com/documentation/hooks/searchwp-index-tokens_max/)
- [ `searchwp\index\process\enabled` ](https://searchwp.com/documentation/hooks/searchwp-index-process-enabled/)
- [ `searchwp\index\aggressive_delta` ](https://searchwp.com/documentation/hooks/searchwp-index-aggressive_delta/)
- [ `searchwp\index\rebuild` ](https://searchwp.com/documentation/hooks/searchwp-index-rebuild/)

---

**Published:** March 5, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Published:** March 5, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

### [v3](https://searchwp.com/v3/)

**Published:** March 5, 2020
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

---

### [Highlighting](https://searchwp.com/v3/docs/settings/highlight/)

**Published:** February 12, 2019
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

## Highlighting Results

When term highlighting is enabled on the [Advanced tab](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/actions-settings/) of the SearchWP settings screen, search terms in the title and **default** excerpt of results will be wrapped in a `mark` tag with a class of `searchwp-highlight`, like so:

`soccer`

Web browsers have a default styling for the `mark` tag, but you can override these styles in any way you see fit.

- [Better (global) excerpts](#better-excerpts)
- [Supplemental engines](#supplemental)
- [Hooks](#hooks)

### Better excerpts

By default, enabling this feature will not change excerpts, but highlighting will take place when a search term is found. SearchWP can retrieve a better excerpt that contains the submitted search term and in doing so override the default excerpt with something that has the highlighted term.

This functionality takes into consideration **Custom Fields** and **Shortcode** output! It will also consider the post content itself much like native excerpt functionality if necessary.

To have SearchWP find a better excerpt on search results pages you can add the following to your themeâs `functions.php`:

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       <?php        function searchwp\_term\_highlight\_auto\_excerpt( $excerpt ) {     global $post;         if ( ! is\_search() ) {     return $excerpt;     }         // prevent recursion     remove\_filter( 'get\_the\_excerpt', 'searchwp\_term\_highlight\_auto\_excerpt' );         $global\_excerpt = searchwp\_term\_highlight\_get\_the\_excerpt\_global( $post->ID, null, get\_search\_query() );         add\_filter( 'get\_the\_excerpt', 'searchwp\_term\_highlight\_auto\_excerpt' );         return wp\_kses\_post( $global\_excerpt );    }        add\_filter( 'get\_the\_excerpt', 'searchwp\_term\_highlight\_auto\_excerpt' );  

 [view raw](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/d3f435c0d2759dd7c0ae60197f5ad787/raw/4618379f251202074bd32fa0af2f08bac647ab6b/functions.php) [ functions.php ](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/d3f435c0d2759dd7c0ae60197f5ad787#file-functions-php) hosted with â¤ by [GitHub](https://github.com) 

This snippet will modify the content returned by calls to `the_excerpt()` so as to include highlights.

If instead you want to manually replace the calls you can use something like this:

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       <?php        /\* SearchWP Term Highlight offers an alternative to the\_excerpt()     If you're looking to generate any sort of excerpt based on one of the following:     - the actual excerpt     - the generated excerpt from the post content     - the post content itself     - string-based custom field values     this function is for you. It will try to find one of the above (in that order)     and output something with at least one search term in it (if possible)    \*/        // echo the excerpt (designed to be used IN PLACE OF the\_excerpt    if( function\_exists( 'searchwp\_term\_highlight\_the\_excerpt\_global' ) ) {     searchwp\_term\_highlight\_the\_excerpt\_global();    }        // return the excerpt (designed to be used IN PLACE OF get\_the\_excerpt    if( function\_exists( 'searchwp\_term\_highlight\_get\_the\_excerpt\_global' ) ) {     $excerpt = searchwp\_term\_highlight\_get\_the\_excerpt\_global();    }  

 [view raw](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/9667991/raw/ae60eaa35cbbcc9f88f60957561b930457daf3b0/gistfile1.php) [ gistfile1.php ](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/9667991#file-gistfile1-php) hosted with â¤ by [GitHub](https://github.com) 

### Supplemental Search Engines

**Term Highlight does not automatically highlight terms for Supplemental Search Engine searches!** You will need to customize your template by sending your content through the highlighter before outputting it, like so:

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       <?php        // this snippet is based on SearchWP's sample Supplemental Search Engine results template found here:    // https://searchwp.com/docs/configuration/#supplemental            // \*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\* NOTE \*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*    // this snippet is just a portion of your search results template    // \*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\* NOTE \*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*\*        // these are the search terms    $query = isset( $\_REQUEST\['swpquery'\] ) ? sanitize\_text\_field( $\_REQUEST\['swpquery'\] ) : '';        $highlighter = false;    if( class\_exists( 'SearchWPHighlighter' ) ) {     $highlighter = new SearchWPHighlighter();    }        ?>        <?php foreach ( $posts as $post ): setup\_postdata( $post ); ?>     <div class="post">     <h2>     <a href="<?php echo get\_permalink(); ?>">     <?php     // highlight the title     $title = get\_the\_title();     if( $highlighter ) {     $title = $highlighter->apply\_highlight( $title, $query );     }     echo wp\_kses\_post( $title );     ?>     </a>     </h2>     <?php      // output an excerpt     $excerpt = get\_the\_excerpt();     if( $highlighter ) {     $excerpt = $highlighter->apply\_highlight( $excerpt, $query );     }     echo wp\_kses\_post( $excerpt );     ?>     <div class="custom-field-content">     <?php      // output highlighted content from a Custom Field     $custom\_field = get\_post\_meta( get\_the\_ID(), 'my\_custom\_field\_key', true );     if( $highlighter ) {     $custom\_field = $highlighter->apply\_highlight( $custom\_field, $query );     }     echo wp\_kses\_post( $custom\_field );     ?>     </div>     </div>    <?php endforeach; ?>  

 [view raw](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/2c35c1f3ef605ee51d87c70b90753f52/raw/f13dec3bbfb38e5976ade46f1390a2f47569a9e0/functions.php) [ functions.php ](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/2c35c1f3ef605ee51d87c70b90753f52#file-functions-php) hosted with â¤ by [GitHub](https://github.com) 

**NOTE:** the snippet above is meant to represent the customization you would need to do to the sample Supplemental Search Engine results template *replacing the search results output ONLY*, it is not an entire page template.

### Hooks

There are a number of hooks to customize the behavior of highlighting in SearchWP:

**`searchwp_th_auto_filter_excerpt`**
Set whether Term Highlight *automatically* replaces `the_excerpt()` with an excerpt containing search terms. Default is **true**, to disable:

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       <?php        add\_filter( 'searchwp\_th\_auto\_filter\_excerpt', '\_\_return\_false' );

### [Search Advanced Custom Fields Data in WordPress](https://searchwp.com/search-acf-advanced-custom-fields-data-in-wordpress/)

**Published:** January 10, 2018
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

[![Advanced Custom Fields Logo](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/advanced-custom-fields-300x147.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/advanced-custom-fields.png)

Everybody loves ACF (Advanced Custom Fields)! 

With **over 1 million active installs**, not only is it arguably the most popular custom field plugin for WordPress, itâs one of the most popular WordPress plugins ever made. Thatâs not without good reason:

- The user interface is great
- The updates are constant
- The community is huge (and helpful)

Working with Custom Fields has become an essential part of establishing WordPress as a CMS as opposed to a simple blogging platform, and ACF has spearheaded that effort by lowering the barrier to entry for millions of websites.

## WordPress does not search Custom Fields!

While ACF does a fantastic job of utilizing WordPressâ Custom Fields (post meta) for structured data storage, **WordPress does not search any of that content!**

Many websites built by WordPress that use ACF put the vast majority of content in Custom Fields (and *not* the main editor) and for good reason, it provides a much nicer interface to work with and reduce error.

Unfortunately a byproduct of this means that hardly any of your site content is considered when performing searches. **Thatâs a huge problem!**

## SearchWP â¤ï¸ ACF

SearchWP was built on the knowledge that this problem plagues many WordPress sites that use Advanced Custom Fields.

Sure, there are ways to `JOIN` WordPress post metadata with the main search query, but performance instantly suffers. The problem grows exponentially as you make more liberal use of ACF (which we all do!)

SearchWP abstracts ACFâs data while building its index, creating an optimized search environment that not only includes all of your Custom Field data in searches, you can also weight individual ACF fields!

[![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-acf-advanced-customf-fields-simple.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-acf-advanced-customf-fields-simple.png)Easily add individual Advanced Custom Fields to your engine configuration!

### ACF Repeaters and Sub-fields: no problem!

Advanced Custom Fields success is based on its many versatile field types. Some field types have a data structure that can sometimes be challenging to work with when attempting to manually `JOIN` in a native WordPress search. SearchWP allows you to define custom meta keys with wildcards. This makes it *super* easy to accommodate an entire Repeater group for instance:

[![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-acf-advanced-custom-fields-repeater.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-acf-advanced-custom-fields-repeater.png)Easily group ACF Repeater fields in SearchWP

Adding these custom meta keys is as easy as adding the following to your themeâs `functions.php` and then adding the Custom Field to your SearchWP engine:

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       <?php        function my\_searchwp\_acf\_repeater\_keys( $keys ) {     $keys\[\] = 'staff\_%'; // will match all ACF Repeater fields in the Staff repeater         return $keys;    }        add\_filter( 'searchwp\_custom\_field\_keys', 'my\_searchwp\_acf\_repeater\_keys' );  

 [view raw](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/ab58242848a9eb076459e7cd40b1b6a5/raw/9ff01ae54ce9bcbf1e8b918c0020ed0043a120d7/functions.php) [ functions.php ](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/ab58242848a9eb076459e7cd40b1b6a5#file-functions-php) hosted with â¤ by [GitHub](https://github.com) 

## SearchWP supports pre-processing data

One of Advanced Custom Fields popular fields is a Relationship field. This field type stores an array of post IDs that arenât very useful when it comes to searching. Usually when a Relationship field is used you instead want to search the *actual* content of the chosen entries stored in the field.

This is easily done with SearchWP because you can integrate custom processing into SearchWPâs indexing process! In this example we can perform some additional processing of the Relationship field content by utilizing SearchWPâs [`searchwp_custom_field_{name}`](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_custom_field/) hook.

Letâs say we have a Relationship field and instead of indexing the post ID as stored by ACF we instead want to have SearchWP index the post Title. We can do that easily by adding the following to our themeâs `functions.php` and rebuilding our index:

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       <?php        function my\_searchwp\_acf\_relationship\_processor( $existing\_value, $the\_post ) {         if ( ! is\_array( $existing\_value ) ) {     return $existing\_value;     }         // We want to index Titles not IDs     $content\_to\_index = '';         // Iterate over stored ACF Relationship field and retrieve Title for each item     foreach ( $existing\_value as $related\_post\_data ) {     if ( is\_numeric( $related\_post\_data ) ) {     $post\_title = get\_the\_title( absint( $related\_post\_data ) );     $content\_to\_index .= $post\_title . ' ';     } else {     $related\_post\_data = maybe\_unserialize( $related\_post\_data );     if ( is\_array( $related\_post\_data ) && ! empty( $related\_post\_data ) ) {     foreach ( $related\_post\_data as $related\_post\_id ) {     $post\_title = get\_the\_title( absint( $related\_post\_id ) );     $content\_to\_index .= $post\_title . ' ';     }     }     }     }         // Instead of indexing the original ACF field value (likely array of IDs)     // we will instead be indexing a string of Titles for each chosen post     return $content\_to\_index;    }        // Adds additional processing for ACF field with the name my\_acf\_relationship\_field    add\_filter( 'searchwp\_custom\_field\_my\_acf\_relationship\_field', 'my\_searchwp\_acf\_relationship\_processor', 10, 2 );  

 [view raw](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/51bae1a467ecd7ec4a31bec9f626d7f6/raw/af67e7f9dd862967f0401c79886749b7d491ac4b/functions.php) [ functions.php ](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/51bae1a467ecd7ec4a31bec9f626d7f6#file-functions-php) hosted with â¤ by [GitHub](https://github.com) 

### The skyâs the limit!

SearchWP was built because Advanced Custom Fields changed the game when it comes to WordPress. The content stored therein is structured in a way that can be replicated in SearchWP, allowing you to fine-tune your search configuration to match your ACF setup!

### [Quoted (Phrase, Sentence) Search Support](https://searchwp.com/v3/docs/settings/advanced/quoted-phrase-sentence-search-support/)

**Published:** August 1, 2019
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

**As of version 3.1,** SearchWP supports quoted searches, sometimes called phrase or sentence-based searches. This allows your visitors to add quotes to their searches so as to force exact matches for that quoted string.

For example searching for `coffee mugs` vs. `"coffee mugs"`

To enable this functionality, tick the appropriate checkbox on the Advanced tab of the SearchWP settings screen:

[![Screenshot of SearchWP's advanced settings screen](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-quoted-search-setting.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-quoted-search-setting.png)

When enabled, SearchWP will check search queries for terms wrapped in quotes, and attempt to find *exact matches* for the quoted string. Referencing again the earlier example of `coffee mugs` vs. `"coffee mugs"`:

**`coffee mugs`**In following [how SearchWP works](https://searchwp.com/docs/kb/how-searchwp-works/) SearchWP will first perform a search using `AND` logic, ensuring that all search terms are present. If no results are found in that pass, SearchWP will automatically revert to `OR` logic. This behavior can be customized using [hooks](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/).**`"coffee mugs"`**Because the search string is wrapped in quotes, SearchWP will attempt to find results that have an exact match of `coffee mugs` (note [caveats](#caveats)) when performing a search. If no results are found that have exact matches, the quotes will be removed and search performed again following that pattern.## Notes

By nature, quoted searches restrict/refine search results. This can result in no results being returned. With SearchWP, if no results are found with a quoted search, the algorithm will fall back to performing the search without quotes. A notice is automatically output to indicate the change.

â ï¸ Style (CSS) customization is likely required â ï¸

[![Screenshot of SearchWP's quoted search fallback message](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-quoted-search-fallback-1030x692.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-quoted-search-fallback.png)

If you would like to prevent the automatic output of this notice you can use the [`searchwp_auto_output_revised_search_query`](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_auto_output_revised_search_query/) hook to disable it. Subsequently you can use the [`searchwp_revised_search_query`](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_revised_search_query/) to implement your own in place of the automatic output.

The above screenshot outlines the automatic output in [Twenty Nineteen](https://wordpress.org/themes/twentynineteen/), a default WordPress theme. The following CSS can be added to improve its appearance:

**Note:** (You can add Custom CSS to your site by navigating to Appearance > Customize > Additional CSS in your WordPress Dashboard)

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       .searchwp-revised-search-notice {     margin: 0 calc(10% + 60px) 1em;     padding: 0.6em;     background: #f7f7f7;     border: 1px solid #e7e7e7;     border-radius: 2px;    }        .searchwp-suggested-revision-query {     font-style: italic;     font-weight: bold;    }  

 [view raw](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/764ce18dd0dba053d774c6b6aedf9f1f/raw/bc7bcfe954526853d2bfb642d49049d81a88e2d9/style.css) [ style.css ](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/764ce18dd0dba053d774c6b6aedf9f1f#file-style-css) hosted with â¤ by [GitHub](https://github.com) 

Which results in a notification that better matches the site design:

[![Screenshot of SearchWP's search correction when styled](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-quoted-search-fallback-styled-1030x692.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-quoted-search-fallback-styled.png)

## Caveats

**There are some limitations to consider** when evaluating SearchWPâs exact match implementation. SearchWP is a [token-based search system](https://searchwp.com/docs/kb/indexer-work-tokenizing/) so by nature SearchWPâs index does not contain full details about exact matches throughout its search index. SearchWPâs indexer also allows for content customization during indexing, which can further obscure exact matches in some cases.

As a result, **SearchWP is *not able* to find exact matches** in the following cases:

- [Shortcode-generated content](https://searchwp.com/search-shortcode-content/)
- [Extra metadata](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_extra_metadata/) (although there is [a workaround](#extra-metadata-workaround))
- Custom database table content
- Content modified during indexing

What this means is that SearchWPâs quoted search feature matches content stored in **native post attributes** (e.g. Title, content, etc.), **Custom Fields** (post metadata) in most cases, and **taxonomy terms**.

Because of these limitations, SearchWPâs quoted search support is an *opt-in* feature, requiring you to enable the functionality either by ticking the checkbox on the Advanced tab of the SearchWP settings screen, or using the [`searchwp_allow_quoted_phrase_search`](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_allow_quoted_phrase_search/) hook.

## Workarounds

There are some limited ways to work around the limitations of SearchWPâs quoted search support, as outlined below.

### Extra Metadata Workaround

When SearchWP indexes [Extra metadata](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_extra_metadata/) the data is stored *only in SearchWPâs index*, which means the only record of the data is one that has been tokenized by the indexer.

In order to avoid the issue of SearchWP not being able to de-tokenize the content to perform exact match queries, there is a hook you can use to have SearchWP **persist** your Extra Metadata as actual records in your `postmeta` table: [`searchwp_persist_extra_metadata`](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_persist_extra_metadata/)

This is an **opt-in** behavior, as SearchWP stores Extra Metadata only in its index to keep database usage at a minimum. When using `searchwp_persist_extra_metadata` SearchWP will take a few steps to make it possible to use Extra Metadata to perform quoted searches:

- In *addition to* the SearchWP index, your Extra Metadata will be stored in your `postmeta` database table as well
- You will still need to add your Extra Metadata keys [as per the docs](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_extra_metadata/) (this is important!)
- Persisted Extra Metadata *will not* be indexed twice, it will remain as Extra Metadata as far as the SearchWP index is concerned
- Your Custom Field dropdown keys will exclude these internal storage keys so they wonât be directly available
- When evaluating quoted searches, the Extra Metadata keys that have been added to your engine will be utilized to perform matches against the persisted metadata that now appears in your `postmeta` table

---

### [Search Taxonomy Terms](https://searchwp.com/search-taxonomy-terms/)

**Published:** November 11, 2016
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

Taxonomies in WordPress are most commonly seen as Post Categories and Tags. You can also register your own taxonomies, and many plugins use their own Taxonomies to facilitate a lot of functionality.

**Bonus!** Using [Term Archive Priority](https://searchwp.com/extensions/term-archive-priority/) you can have your taxonomy term archive pages be returned in search results!

[![Screenshot of Categories and Tags](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-12.19-PM.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-12.19-PM.png)Categories and Tags include important search keywords!

Taxonomy terms (e.g. your Categories/Tags themselves) can contain content thatâs very relevant to search! WordPress native search doesnât consider taxonomy terms when performing searches but SearchWP indexes and considers that content.

---

### [Actions & Settings](https://searchwp.com/v3/docs/settings/advanced/actions-settings/)

**Published:** February 12, 2019
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

## Actions & Settings

[Advanced Settings](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/)

The *Actions & Settings* area of the Advanced settings screen allows you to perform common actions and configure popular settings.

**Note** that performing some Actions or toggling some Settings may require follow-up actions (e.g. resetting the index) at which time a notice will be displayed.

  [![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/02-searchwp-advanced-index-reset-index-1030x577.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/02-searchwp-advanced-index-reset-index.png)   [![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/03-searchwp-advanced-parse-shortcodes-1030x610.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/03-searchwp-advanced-parse-shortcodes.png)   [![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/04-searchwp-advanced-exclusive-regex-matches-1030x610.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/04-searchwp-advanced-exclusive-regex-matches.png) 
  [![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/05-searchwp-advanced-partial-matches-1030x610.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/05-searchwp-advanced-partial-matches.png)   [![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/06-searchwp-advanced-min-word-length-1030x610.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/06-searchwp-advanced-min-word-length.png)  

**Reset Index**Reset (empty) the current index. This operation is necessary from time to time, particularly when making changes that require the index to be rebuilt. Once confirmed, the index will be emptied and a rebuild will be triggered in the background.**Wake Up Indexer**If the index progress is not advancing, this button will wake up the indexer in case it has stalled.**Restore Notices**If you have dismissed any notices this button will restore them.### Settings

**Debugging Enabled**Whether [debugging](https://searchwp.com/docs/debugging/) is enabled.**Parse Shortcodes when indexing**Whether [Shortcodes](https://codex.wordpress.org/Shortcode_API) are parsed (expanded, processed) during the indexing process.**Reduced indexer aggressiveness**By default SearchWP will monitor resource usage during indexing, but ticking this box will forcefully reduce how aggressively the index is built**Admin engine**Enable interception of searches in the WordPress Admin (Dashboard) and define the engine used for those searches. *Note: any existing filters in place are **not** applied during searches.***Exclusive regex matches**By default SearchWP will index [pattern matches](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_term_pattern_whitelist/) alongside tokenized versions of those matches. Tick this checkbox to index only the pattern match and *not* the tokens generated from that pattern match.**Use alternate indexer**Whether a browser-based indexer should be used (as opposed to the default background indexer)**Partial matches when applicable**Whether partial matches should be considered for searches. This is a **big deal** and should be enabled only when necessary. [More information on partial matches](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/partial-matches/)**Remove minimum word length**Much like Stopwords, words with [too few characters](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_minimum_word_length/) are omitted from the index (default is 3)**Highlight terms in results**Whether search terms are highlighted in titles/excerpts on search results pages. [More information](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/highlight/)**Automatic âDid you mean?â corrections**When no results are found, attempt to find the closest match automatically. [More information](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/automatic-did-you-mean/)**Support âquoted/phrase searchesâ**Limit results to exact matches of search phrases wrapped in double quotes. [More information (caveats!)](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/quoted-phrase-sentence-search-support/)**Remove all data on uninstall**Whether SearchWP should remove all data (index and all settings) when *deleted* using the Plugins page in the WordPress Admin.

---

### [Synonyms](https://searchwp.com/v3/docs/settings/advanced/synonyms/)

**Published:** February 12, 2019
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

## Synonyms

[Advanced Settings](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/)

Taking time to customize the synonyms used on your website can go a long way in helping visitors find what theyâre looking for. Sometimes your content may be full of industry jargon thatâs sometimes referenced using other words. Other times your content may always refer to one term while your visitors are searching for another. Using synonyms you can customize the search experience for your visitors to ensure that your content is found.

[![Screenshot of synonym handling in SearchWP](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-synonyms-1030x575.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-synonyms.png)

When managing synonyms on your site, enter the *source* search term (e.g. the term that your visitors will enter in your search form) in the **Search Term** field. Next, enter the applicable synonym(s) for that search term in the **Synonym(s)** field.

The **Replace** checkbox controls whether the source **Search Term** is retained when the search takes place. When the Replace checkbox is ticked, the Search Term will be removed for the search. When left unticked, the Synonym(s) will be *appended* to the Search Term.

Synonyms can be sorted using the handles next to the Replace checkbox. New synonyms can be added using the `Add New` button.

## How Synonyms are processed

**Note: this applies to SearchWP version 3.1+**

Synonym processing has been implemented in such a way that its goal is to generate the best search results. Given that, the default processing works as follows:

1. Determine which synonyms to add to the search query
2. Remove applicable removals
3. Rebuild the search query

This implementation is the most generalized and least aggressive way to process synonyms, generating the most comprehensive resulting search string.

There is another synonym processing method built in however, which can be enabled with the [`searchwp_synonyms_aggressive`](https://searchwp.com/docs/hooks/searchwp_synonyms_aggressive/) hook. In contrast to the default processing implementation, this aggressive method will apply synonym replacements as each synonym is processed. This mostly comes into effect when synonyms are directly related to one another in that some synonyms include search terms from other synonyms or vice versa.

The default processing method is recommended, but this more aggressive processing routine is also available if youâd like to use that instead.

---

### [Search E-Commerce Product Details](https://searchwp.com/search-e-commerce-product-details/)

**Published:** November 11, 2016
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

Native WordPress search doesnât allow you to search things like your e-commerce product details, which can severely limit your ability to effectively sell your products!

SearchWP makes it really easy to directly integrate with your e-commerce plugin of choice, making all of your product characteristics easily searchable. SearchWP can index:

- Your products Custom Fields (metadata)
- Your product Taxonomies
- Your product reviews
- Your order history

Naturally this depends on how your e-commerce plugin utilizes Custom Fields, Taxonomies, Comments, orders (and more) but SearchWP has many happy customers using:

- [WooCommerce](https://woocommerce.com/) ([find out more!](https://searchwp.com/better-woocommerce-search-wordpress/))
- [Easy Digital Downloads](https://easydigitaldownloads.com/)
- [BigCommerce for WordPress](https://wordpress.org/plugins/bigcommerce/)
- [WP eCommerce](https://wpecommerce.org/)

***and more!*** If youâre curious about whether SearchWP will work with your WordPress e-commerce plugin, please donât hesitate [to ask](https://searchwp.com/contact/)!

## Take WooCommerce for Example

WooCommerce makes *heavy* use of Custom Fields and Taxonomies as a basis for itâs powerful platform. Take for example your Product Attributes:

[![Screenshot of WooCommerce Attributes](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-11.17-AM.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-11.17-AM.png)WooCommerce Product Attributes are powered by Taxonomies

Product Attributes are powered by WordPress Taxonomies, which are indexed by SearchWP!

[![Screenshot of WooCommerce Product configuration in SearchWP](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-woocommerce-wordpress-1030x615.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-woocommerce-wordpress.png)

WooCommerce also utilizes WordPress Comments for Product reviews, which is great!

[![WooCommerce Reviews are WordPress Comments](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-11.23-AM.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/2016-11-11-at-11.23-AM.png)WooCommerce Reviews are WordPress Comments

SearchWP also allows you to utilize WooCommerce Product Review content in searches on your site.

### Searching WooCommerce Orders

You know itâs important to be able to effectively search existing Orders for any number of reasons. Whether itâs to quickly help a customer looking for support, or perhaps do a bit of research along the way. SearchWP also allows you to easily make your WooCommerce Orders searchable.

For an explanation of integrating SearchWP with your WooCommerce orders please review this Knowledge Base article: [Searching WooCommerce Orders](https://searchwp.com/docs/kb/searching-woocommerce-orders/)

---

### [Search Custom Fields Content](https://searchwp.com/search-custom-fields/)

**Published:** November 11, 2016
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

With plugins like [Advanced Custom Fields](https://www.advancedcustomfields.com/) taking the WordPress world by storm, we are using Custom Fields to power our WordPress-powered websites more than ever before.

SearchWP was built to take into consideration the extensive utilization of Custom Fields (sometimes referred to as *postmeta* or *metadata*) for searches as well!

## What does SearchWP integrate with?

It doesnât matter to SearchWP what plugin you may be using to power your utilization of Custom Fields, it works directly with the [Custom Fields API](https://codex.wordpress.org/Custom_Fields) in the same way your favorite custom fields plugin does.

SearchWP works great with:

- [Advanced Custom Fields](https://wordpress.org/plugins/advanced-custom-fields/) (free and [Pro](https://www.advancedcustomfields.com/pro/)) [Find out more about ACF integration](https://searchwp.com/search-acf-advanced-custom-fields-data-in-wordpress/)
- [Meta Box](https://metabox.io/) and [MB Custom Table](https://metabox.io/plugins/mb-custom-table/) data (see [Meta Box Integration](https://searchwp.com/extensions/meta-box-integration/))
- [CMB2](https://wordpress.org/plugins/cmb2/)
- [Custom Field Suite](https://wordpress.org/plugins/custom-field-suite/)
- [Piklist](https://wordpress.org/plugins/piklist/)
- [Types](https://wordpress.org/plugins/types/)
- ***and many more (probably the one youâre using)!***

If youâre curious if SearchWP will work with your Custom Field plugin, [please ask](https://searchwp.com/contact/)!

### What setup is required?

Integrating your Custom Field content with search results using SearchWP is quick and easy â just add your Custom Fields to your engine configuration and give them a weight. Thatâs it!

[![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-custom-fields.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-custom-fields.png)Fine-grained control over your Custom Fields (per post type!)

***NOTE:** you can add an âAnyâ Custom Field to SearchWPâs engine configuration that accounts for data stored in any Custom Field.*

---

### [Settings](https://searchwp.com/v3/docs/settings/)

**Published:** June 2, 2016
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

# Settings

The primary purpose of the SearchWP settings screen is to allow modification of the weights used within SearchWPâs algorithm. Each search engine has its own settings. The **Default** engine replaces all WordPress native searches.

- [General Settings](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/#general)
    - [Transfer weight to post parent](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/#attribute-post-parent)
    - [Keyword stemming](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/#keyword-stemming)
- [Advanced Settings](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/#advanced)
- [Support](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/#support)

## General Settings

Each Supplemental Engine has the same settings as the default engine, but also allows you to name it. A code-friendly version of your name is provided alongside the engine name, and thatâs what you will use in your code.

Engines are primarily based on post types. You can add/exclude any available post type to any engine at any time. If a post type is listed in the configuration, it will be included in searches.

[![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-features.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-features.png)The main SearchWP settings screen

You can create as many supplemental engines as youâd like. Hereâs a walkthrough on how to use a Supplemental Engine on your site: 

All of the registered (and not excluded from search) post types are available for each SearchWP engine configuration. Media is somewhat unique, however.

[![](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-ui-media.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-ui-media.png)Customizing Media within a SearchWP engine

Note that Document content and PDF metadata each have their own Weight fields which apply only to documents SearchWP was able to parse and index.

#### Transfer weight to parent

The parent attribution feature for Media in SearchWP can be very helpful. By default, SearchWP will return Media results just like any post type. The link will direct you to the Attachment page for that Media (which can be customized to [link directly to the file](https://searchwp.com/docs/kb/link-file-instead-attachment-page/) if youâd like) but thatâs not always desirable.

With **Transfer weight to parent** enabled, SearchWP will instead return the parent post for Media search results. For example, if you have a Page on your site, and youâve uploaded a PDF to that Page while editing that Page (i.e. WordPress lists that PDF as **Uploaded to** that Page) searches for content within that PDF will return the parent Page as the result instead of the PDF itself.

Find out more about **Transfer weight to parent** in this KB article: [Media: Post Parent Attribution](https://searchwp.com/docs/kb/post-parent-attribution/)

#### Use Keyword Stem

Keyword stemming is an active process based on the language of the term itself. Terms are checked for a suffix (e.g. âingâ, âedâ, etc.) and the base of the word is determined and used as a proper stem. For a detailed explanation of keyword stemming please see [this Wikipedia article](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Word_stem).

For example:

**baseball** does not have a stem of **base** (in fact it has no stem at all)

**fishing** does have a stem of **fish**

Tick this checkbox to enable this sort of behavior when performing searches.

## Advanced Settings

The Advanced tab of the SearchWP settings screen allows you to take some common actions that may prove to be useful from time to time. [Read more](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/).

[![Screenshot of SearchWP Advanced settings](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-settings-1-1030x623.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-advanced-settings-1.png)

For more information please see the [Advanced settings screen documentation](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/).

## Support

You can use the Support tab of the SearchWP settings screen to open a support ticket. An active license is required to open a support ticket.

Please begin by using a few words to describe your issue, which will guide you through the SearchWP documentation search. If the documentation does not help, youâll be able to create a ticket right there.

[![Screenshot of SearchWP's Support screen](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-support-tab-1030x987.png)](https://searchwp.com/wp-content/uploads/searchwp-support-tab.png)

Below that is your System Information, which includes some details about your server and WordPress install, along with your SearchWP engine settings. This can sometimes be useful when troubleshooting a support ticket, so please be prepared to [provide a copy](https://privatebin.searchwp.com/) upon request from SearchWP support.

---

### [Partial matches](https://searchwp.com/v3/docs/settings/partial-matches/)

**Published:** February 12, 2019
**Author:** Jonathan Christopher (main)

**Content:**

## Partial matches

Enabling partial matches when applicable on SearchWPâs Advanced settings screen will tell SearchWP to be more lenient when finding search results. This behavior is **different** than enabling [keyword stemming](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/#keyword-stemming) and as a result:

**â ï¸ This feature should be enabled *with caution* â ï¸**

SearchWP is a [token based search system](https://searchwp.com/docs/kb/how-searchwp-works/). It will break apart your content into tokens and those tokens are used when performing searches. Tokens are separated by white space and punctuation. Because of the strictness of this approach SearchWP is able to improve both relevance and speed of your WordPress search.

Another byproduct of a tokenized search is sometimes not meeting expectations when certain search terms are submitted. As a result, SearchWP is *very* extensible and one way to modify SearchWPâs behavior is by enabling partial term matches where applicable.

### Partial matches can reduce relevance

Many times customers will enable partial term matching as a preemptive catch-all to prevent possible no results searches. Unfortunately this can introduce some completely unrelated terms (that satisfy a requirement of partial matches) into the algorithm, which can reduce the relevance of the results youâre seeing.

### A better initial approach

Because introducing partial matches can have an adverse effect on the overall relevance of search results, it is suggested that you instead monitor your search statistics to determine what visitors are searching for. Pay particular attention to the No Results Searches and add appropriate [Synonyms](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/synonyms/) for those failed searches.

This approach will allow you to retain the relevancy of SearchWPâs algorithm, but also solve the issue of misspellings and partial matches on a case-by-case basis.

If on your website there are far too many failed searches and it is not feasible to maintain an ever-growing list of [Synonyms](https://searchwp.com/docs/settings/advanced/synonyms/) to combat failed searches, enabling partial matches where applicable may be a better solution.

### How partial matching works

Please review the following KB article: [How Partial Matching Works](https://searchwp.com/docs/kb/how-partial-matching-works/)

### Hooks

There are a number of filters to customize your partial match implementation:

**`searchwp_partial_matching_{$engine}`**
Customize whether partial matching is enabled per engine. For example:

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       <?php        // Note that 'my\_supplemental\_engine' is the name of the engine    // This will prevent partial matching for the my\_supplemental\_engine engine    // but leave partial matching enabled (when the checkbox is ticked) for all others    add\_filter( 'searchwp\_partial\_matching\_my\_supplemental\_engine', '\_\_return\_false' );  

 [view raw](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/e815a45efc840beab1776f07ab3d2903/raw/231abae122b0dbb9f69ce0434a1a179be1c57a21/functions.php) [ functions.php ](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/e815a45efc840beab1776f07ab3d2903#file-functions-php) hosted with â¤ by [GitHub](https://github.com) 

**`searchwp_partial_matching_aggressive`**
By default SearchWP *will* find partial matches if there is an exact match in the search query. You can change that with this hook like so:

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       <?php        function searchwp\_partial\_matching\_aggressive( $return, $args ) {     // $args contains information about this request     // - engine          // TODO: customize $return to match whether matching should be aggressive     return $return;    }        add\_filter( 'searchwp\_partial\_matching\_aggressive', 'my\_searchwp\_partial\_matching\_aggressive', 10, 2 );  

 [view raw](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/c8ff57866961bc18cc488e54f2514f58/raw/c689be27ae97df8a3f4ad2cbcbb9d8e3385a7a07/functions.php) [ functions.php ](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/c8ff57866961bc18cc488e54f2514f58#file-functions-php) hosted with â¤ by [GitHub](https://github.com) 

**`searchwp_partial_matches_lenient`**
Using this hook you can tell SearchWP to *NOT* find partial matches if there are exact matches in the search query.

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       <?php        function searchwp\_partial\_matches\_lenient( $return, $args ) {     // $args contains information about this request     // - engine          // TODO: customize $return to match whether matching should be lenient     return $return;    }        add\_filter( 'searchwp\_partial\_matches\_lenient', 'my\_searchwp\_partial\_matches\_lenient', 10, 2 );  

 [view raw](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/ad2b6d128ad3c91bdfcd964cfdc4a659/raw/12a827cd0bbf228d4cc5e6a3037de508f1cdfc47/functions.php) [ functions.php ](https://gist.github.com/jchristopher/ad2b6d128ad3c91bdfcd964cfdc4a659#file-functions-php) hosted with â¤ by [GitHub](https://github.com) 

**`searchwp_partial_matches_force_fuzzy`**
By default SearchWP will locate partial matches and only if none are found it will perform fuzzy matching to broaden the criteria for partial matches.

     This file contains hidden or bidirectional Unicode text that may be interpreted or compiled differently than what appears below. To review, open the file in an editor that reveals hidden Unicode characters. [Learn more about bidirectional Unicode characters](https://github.co/hiddenchars)  [ Show hidden characters ](%7B%7B%20revealButtonHref%20%7D%7D)

       <?php        function my\_searchwp\_partial\_matches\_force\_fuzzy( $return, $args ) {     // $args contains information about this request     // - engine          // TODO: customize $return to match whether fuzzy logic should be forced      // when partial matches are already found     return $return;    }        add\_filter( 'searchwp\_partial\_matches\_force\_fuzzy', 'my\_searchwp\_partial\_matches\_force\_fuzzy', 10, 2 );
